title: Digital Skills Framework — Weston College (Full Curriculum Edition)

tab1_generalSkills.title: General Digital Skills
tab1_generalSkills.intro: Drawn from the DfE Essential Digital Skills Framework and the FutureDotNow AI-updated EDS (2025), these skills are organised across three contexts: learning, work, and societal inclusion. The five core EDS categories — Communicating, Handling Information & Content, Transacting, Problem Solving, and Being Safe & Legal Online — cut across all three contexts.
tab1_generalSkills.infoBox: Why this matters at Weston College: The EIF Toolkit explicitly states that learners must develop "the digital skills needed to study curriculum and learning goals." These 15 foundational skills represent the floor — the minimum every learner should reach regardless of their programme.

skills.name: Navigating Digital Platforms
skills.description: Using VLEs, Teams, SharePoint; accessing learning resources and timetables online.

skills.name: Research & Information Literacy
skills.description: Searching effectively, evaluating sources, avoiding misinformation and AI-generated errors.

skills.name: Digital Note-taking & Organisation
skills.description: OneNote, cloud documents, folder structures, and version control for assignments.

skills.name: Collaborative Digital Working
skills.description: Shared documents, group tasks in Teams or Google Workspace, peer review online.

skills.name: AI-Assisted Learning
skills.description: Using AI tools ethically for research, drafting and self-quizzing without plagiarism.

skills.name: Professional Communication
skills.description: Email etiquette, Teams/Zoom calls, digital messaging in workplace contexts.

skills.name: Productivity & Office Applications
skills.description: Word processing, spreadsheets, presentations and scheduling tools (Microsoft/Google).

skills.name: Digital Transactions & Invoicing
skills.description: Online payments, basic invoicing apps, managing work-related digital finances.

skills.name: Data Handling & Record-Keeping
skills.description: Entering, checking and interpreting workplace data; compliant digital record-keeping.

skills.name: AI Literacy for the Workplace
skills.description: Using AI assistants, understanding outputs critically, prompt skills and ethical use.

skills.name: Accessing Public & Government Services
skills.description: HMRC, Universal Credit, NHS booking, local council portals and digital identity.

skills.name: Online Safety & Privacy
skills.description: Password management, recognising scams, two-factor authentication, GDPR basics.

skills.name: Digital Financial Inclusion
skills.description: Online banking, comparing services, avoiding fraud, energy switching portals.

skills.name: Critical Engagement with Media
skills.description: Identifying misinformation, understanding algorithmic bias, responsible social media use.

skills.name: Digital Wellbeing & Accessibility
skills.description: Screen time awareness, assistive technology, designing/using accessible digital content.

sources.title: DfE Essential Digital Skills Framework
sources.description: Government · 5 core categories · Life & Work standards

sources.title: FutureDotNow — AI-Updated EDS Framework 2025
sources.description: Adds AI literacy, prompt engineering & ethical AI use

sources.title: Ipsos / Lloyds Banking Essential Digital Skills 2024
sources.description: Annual tracking of UK workforce digital capability

sources.title: Good Things Foundation — Basic Digital Skills Briefing

tab4_framing.title: Framing Digital Skills for Industry Needs
tab4_framing.intro: How digital skills are framed determines whether staff and learners engage or disengage. The most effective framing connects directly to an industry outcome learners already care about.
tab4_framing.principles: Never teach the technology. Teach the task that technology enables. A construction learner doesn't need to learn 'how to use an app.' They need to clock in, submit a site diary, order materials, and read a drawing. The apps are just how they do it.

frames.title: The Industry Standard Frame
frames.description: Frame digital skills as standard professional practice — not technology training, but what every employer expects on day one.
frames.example:
Construction: "Every major contractor uses digital clock-in. You won't get paid if you can't sign in on site digitally."
Engineering: "EV diagnostics are entirely digital — you can't fix a modern vehicle without a tablet."

frames.title: The Time-Saving Frame
frames.description: Frame digital tools as reducing admin burden for tasks learners will have to do anyway — invoicing, booking, recording, communicating.
frames.example:
Hair & Beauty: "Fresha sends reminders automatically and takes deposits. You spend less time chasing no-shows."
Health & Care: "Electronic records mean you don't rewrite the same patient information by hand three times a shift."

frames.title: The Employability & Earnings Frame
frames.description: Link digital competence directly to higher pay, better job prospects, or self-employment viability.
frames.example:
Business: "Xero certification adds £3-5k to your starting salary as a bookkeeper."
Performing Arts: "Casting directors now expect digital self-tapes. You won't get seen without knowing how to film and upload professionally."

frames.title: The Authentic Context Frame
frames.description: Teach digital skills inside real vocational tasks rather than as separate lessons. The digital skill becomes invisible — the learner is just doing their job.
frames.example:
Construction: "Measure this wall, log the data, and email it to the project manager." — learner has just done data entry, file handling, and email in one task.
ESol: "Book a GP appointment on the NHS app." — learner is navigating a real digital service, not a classroom simulation.

tab6_progression.title: Digital Skills Progression — Basic to Advanced
tab6_progression.intro: Two parallel ladders showing progression from absolute beginner to highly advanced — one for General Digital Skills (applicable to all citizens and learners) and one for IT & Computing Skills specific to FE & HE contexts. Both are structured using the DigComp 3.0 proficiency levels (European Commission, November 2025).

ladders.name: General Digital Skills
ladders.subtitle: For all citizens, learners & employees · Based on DfE EDS Framework & DigComp 3.0

skills.title: Switching on & basic navigation
skills.description: Can switch on a device, use a mouse or touchscreen, open an app or browser. Needs guidance for most tasks.

skills.title: Recognising common icons & interfaces
skills.description: Identifies common icons (Wi-Fi, battery, settings). Can locate and tap familiar apps used regularly.

skills.title: Basic internet browsing
skills.description: Can open a browser and type a URL or search term. May not distinguish between a search result and an advertisement.

skills.title: Sending and receiving basic messages
skills.description: Can send a text message or WhatsApp. Struggles with email attachments or creating a new contact from scratch.

skills.title: Email — composing, replying, attaching
skills.description: Can write, send, reply and forward emails. Can attach a document. Knows not to open suspicious attachments.

skills.title: Searching effectively online
skills.description: Uses search engines with specific terms. Can open multiple tabs, bookmark pages, and navigate back.

skills.title: Creating simple documents
skills.description: Can open Word or Google Docs, type, edit, and save. Applies basic formatting (bold, font size, bullet lists).

skills.title: Basic online transactions
skills.description: Can shop online, fill in a digital form, and create an account. Understands passwords and uses one for accounts.

skills.title: Accessing public digital services
skills.description: Can navigate gov.uk, book a GP appointment online, or use the NHS App.

skills.title: Productive use of office applications
skills.description: Uses Word, Excel (basic formulae, sorting, simple charts), and PowerPoint confidently. Creates structured documents with headings.

skills.title: Video calling and digital collaboration
skills.description: Joins and hosts video calls (Teams, Zoom). Uses shared documents simultaneously with others. Manages files across folders and cloud storage independently.

skills.title: Online safety — active practice
skills.description: Uses unique strong passwords and a password manager. Recognises phishing emails. Enables two-factor authentication.

skills.title: Evaluating digital information critically
skills.description: Checks publication dates, author credentials, and cross-references sources. Recognises misinformation patterns.

skills.title: Creating and sharing digital content
skills.description: Produces presentations, short videos, infographics, or social posts for a defined purpose. Understands copyright and Creative Commons basics.

skills.title: Basic AI tool use
skills.description: Can use AI assistants (ChatGPT, Copilot) to draft text, summarise documents, or answer questions. Knows to verify AI outputs.

skills.title: Advanced data management & analysis
skills.description: Uses Excel pivot tables, VLOOKUP, and IF statements. Can build basic Power BI dashboards. Manages datasets, cleans data, and produces evidence-based reports.

skills.title: Digital project management
skills.description: Uses project management tools (Planner, Trello, Asana). Manages workflows, assigns tasks digitally, tracks progress, and reports on outcomes.

skills.title: AI as a working tool
skills.description: Uses AI strategically to accelerate work — writing, research, summarising, coding. Evaluates AI outputs critically.

skills.title: Digital communication strategy
skills.description: Manages professional digital presence (LinkedIn). Uses digital channels strategically for audience-appropriate communication.

skills.title: Cybersecurity — personal and organisational
skills.description: Understands GDPR obligations. Recognises social engineering attacks. Manages security settings across devices and platforms.

skills.title: Strategic digital leadership
skills.description: Shapes digital culture, policy or strategy at organisational or sector level. Advocates for digital inclusion and accessibility.

skills.title: Building and automating digital workflows
skills.description: Uses low-code / no-code tools (Power Automate, Zapier) to automate routine tasks. Creates systems others rely on.

skills.title: Advanced AI and digital innovation
skills.description: Identifies novel applications of AI and digital tools in their field. Builds custom GPTs or AI workflows.

skills.title: Digital rights, ethics and advocacy
skills.description: Understands societal implications of digital technology — algorithmic bias, data sovereignty, digital rights.

tab7_teacherSkills.title: Teacher Digital Skills (TLA-Focused Progression)
tab7_teacherSkills.intro: A structured progression framework for FE & HE teaching staff — from first engaging with technology in the classroom to leading digital innovation across the college.

stages.audience: Newly appointed staff · Career changers · Low-confidence digital users

skills.name: Accessing and using the VLE / LMS
skills.description: Can log into the college VLE, view existing course content, and upload a simple resource. Understands how learners access the same space.

skills.name: Using Microsoft Teams for communication
skills.description: Can join a Teams meeting, post to a channel, share a file, and message a learner or colleague.

skills.name: Creating basic digital presentations
skills.description: Produces PowerPoint slides with text and images for classroom use. Understands the difference between a slide deck used in class and one shared with learners online.

skills.name: Submitting admin data digitally
skills.description: Can complete register, funding, and compliance forms on college MIS systems. Submits data accurately and understands GDPR basics.

skills.name: Aware of digital tools available in the college
skills.description: Has attended an overview of the college's key digital tools. Knows which tools are available and approved.

stages.audience: Staff with 1–2 years experience · Those completing or post-ITE

skills.name: Structuring an active VLE course space
skills.description: Has built a well-organised VLE course with clear sections, resources for each topic, and learner-facing instructions. Learners can independently navigate the space.

skills.name: Using Teams for active learner engagement
skills.description: Uses Teams channels for ongoing learner discussion, Q&A, and feedback. Sets Teams assignments with rubrics and due dates. Uses breakout rooms for small-group work.

skills.name: Formative digital assessment
skills.description: Uses digital tools for formative assessment — Microsoft Forms quizzes, Mentimeter, Kahoot, or Padlet for checking understanding within lessons.

skills.name: Creating accessible digital resources
skills.description: Applies accessibility principles to all digital materials — readable fonts, sufficient colour contrast, alt-text on images, headings in Word documents. Uses Microsoft Office's Accessibility Checker.

skills.name: Using data from college systems to inform teaching
skills.description: Reviews attendance, attainment, and target data from ProMonitor. Identifies at-risk learners digitally and records intervention.

skills.name: Ethical AI awareness in TLA
skills.description: Understands how learners are using AI tools (ChatGPT, Copilot) in their work. Has a position on AI in assessment. Can identify likely AI-generated content.

stages.audience: Confident practitioners · Those seeking ATS status · Staff mentoring newer colleagues

skills.name: Technology-enhanced active learning design
skills.description: Designs lesson sequences where digital tools purposefully change the learning experience — enabling collaboration, problem-solving, or creative production. Applies frameworks like 21CLD or UDL.

skills.name: Digital differentiation
skills.description: Uses digital tools to personalise learning at scale — adaptive quiz tools, choice boards created digitally, differentiated resources shared via the VLE for different ability groups.

skills.name: Producing and editing digital media for teaching
skills.description: Produces screencasts (Loom, OBS), short instructional videos, or narrated PowerPoints for flipped learning. Captions all video content.

skills.name: Advanced digital assessment and feedback
skills.description: Uses Turnitin originality reports and rubrics. Provides audio or video feedback on learner work. Uses digital annotation tools (DocHub, Adobe) on submitted PDFs.

skills.name: AI as a teaching productivity tool
skills.description: Uses AI tools (Microsoft Copilot, MagicSchool.ai) to draft lesson plans, generate differentiated resources, produce quiz banks, and summarise learner feedback. Critically evaluates AI outputs.

skills.name: Building learners' digital skills in subject context
skills.description: Deliberately plans for learners to develop digital skills within the subject — not as a bolt-on, but embedded in vocational tasks. Maps which EDS framework categories their learners are developing.

stages.audience: Digital Champions · TLAMs with digital remit · Heads of Area · Those pursuing ATS

skills.name: Mentoring and coaching digital practice
skills.description: Supports and coaches colleagues on digital tools and pedagogy — not just demonstrating but modelling reflective practice. Identifies where a colleague is on the DTPF progression and tailors coaching.

skills.name: Designing digital-first curriculum
skills.description: Designs entire units or programmes where digital tools are integral to the learning journey. Assessment strategy incorporates digital evidence. Learners build digital skills explicitly mapped to the EDS Framework.

skills.name: Critical evaluation and adoption of EdTech
skills.description: Evaluates new educational technology critically — pedagogical rationale, accessibility compliance, data privacy (GDPR), cost, and sustainability. Makes evidence-based recommendations on EdTech adoption.

skills.name: AI pedagogy leadership
skills.description: Leads the team's thinking on AI in teaching and assessment — drafts or contributes to the area's AI policy, models ethical AI use, supports colleagues in navigating the AI landscape.

skills.name: Digital research and innovation in practice
skills.description: Conducts or participates in action research into digital pedagogy. Writes up or presents findings — in team meetings, at sector CPD events, or via publication.

skills.name: Systemic digital inclusion advocacy
skills.description: Ensures digital accessibility is embedded at curriculum and resource level across the team. Audits the team's VLE provision for WCAG compliance. Champions assistive technology use for SEND learners.
